glossary.def: The average surprisal of a random source, H(p) = -Σ p(x) log p(x). It measures uncertainty in bits (log base 2) and is the irreducible lower bound on how many bits per symbol any lossless code can use. Maximal for the uniform distribution, zero for a point mass.

The self-information of a single outcome, I(x) = -log₂ p(x), measured in bits. Rare events carry high surprisal; certainties carry zero. It is the optimal codeword length for that outcome under source coding.

The expected number of bits to encode data drawn from p using a code optimized for a model q: H(p,q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x). It equals the data's own entropy plus the KL divergence of q from p, and is the loss function minimized when training neural classifiers and language models.

Relative entropy, D_KL(p‖q) = Σ p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)), the number of wasted bits from using model q when the truth is p. It is non-negative, zero only when q = p (Gibbs' inequality), and asymmetric — not a true distance.

I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X), the reduction in uncertainty about one variable from observing another. It equals the KL divergence between the joint distribution and the product of marginals, is symmetric, and is zero exactly when X and Y are independent — capturing nonlinear dependence that correlation misses.

The exponential of the cross-entropy, PPL = b^H(p,q), interpreted as the effective number of equally likely choices a model faces per step. A fair coin has perplexity 2; a perfect predictor has perplexity 1. The standard intrinsic metric for language models.

Shannon's result that a source with entropy H needs at least H bits per symbol to encode losslessly, and can be compressed arbitrarily close to H. Entropy is therefore a hard compression limit no algorithm can beat; Huffman coding constructively approaches it.

The evidence lower bound, a tractable lower bound on the log-likelihood used to train VAEs and diffusion models: a reconstruction term minus the KL divergence of the approximate posterior from the prior. Maximizing it is again pure information-theoretic bookkeeping.

refs.cite: Shannon, C. E. (1948). A Mathematical Theory of Communication. Bell System Technical Journal 27.
Kullback, S. & Leibler, R. A. (1951). On Information and Sufficiency. Annals of Mathematical Statistics 22(1).
Cover, T. M. & Thomas, J. A. (2006). Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed.). Wiley.
Huffman, D. A. (1952). A Method for the Construction of Minimum-Redundancy Codes. Proceedings of the IRE 40(9).
MacKay, D. J. C. (2003). Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms. Cambridge University Press.
Kingma, D. P. & Welling, M. (2014). Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes. ICLR.
Tishby, N., Pereira, F. C. & Bialek, W. (2000). The Information Bottleneck Method.
